words: SAM SCHRAGER: Where did come from?
WILLIAM MORGAN: Kansas City. From Kansas.

speaker: SS
words: With your folks?

speaker: WM
words: Yeah. My folks sold out Kansas. I was born what is now the center

speaker: WM
words: of Fort Riley. We had a homestead there, right in the center of the

speaker: WM
words: present Fort Riley, which is ten miles square. And then we landed up

speaker: WM
words: Kootenay Lake, up on the Kootenay River. And my dad had sold out there

speaker: WM
words: Kansas because of a big thunder storm. He had quite a farm

speaker: WM
words: and he run stock scales and bought and sold stock. And a big rain storm

speaker: WM
words: come up one forenoon when he'd received a carload of hogs and the rain

speaker: WM
words: soaked the ground and the next morning cockleburs came up, which are

speaker: WM
words: poison and poisoned practically all of this carload of hogs and he just

speaker: WM
words: sold out his two threshing outfits, everything he had and moved out

speaker: WM
words: West here. We landed at Kootenay and stayed there that summer while

speaker: WM
words: he looked the reservation over here for a homestead. He finally bought

speaker: WM
words: a relinquishment and we moved down here that summer and stayed at what

speaker: WM
words: was known at the old De France Hotel. And that's quite a historic spot.

speaker: SS
words: Do you remember it at that time?

speaker: WM
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: SS
words: Was it quite a place at that time?

speaker: WM
words: My mother and my brother and myself stayed there about two months that

speaker: WM
words: summer while my dad went over and finished getting ready to move up to

speaker: WM
words: the prairie; Nez Perce Prairie. We moved to Moler, Moler, Idaho. Which

speaker: WM
words: at that time became quite a town. There's nothing there but a windmill.

speaker: SS
words: Where was this near now?

speaker: WM
words: Moler, Idaho?

speaker: SS
words: Yeah. Well, it s between Craigmont and Nez Perce; about half way.

speaker: SS
words: At that time a man by the name of Moler had made arrangements and sur

speaker: SS
words: veyed the railroad from the Clearwater up a little canyon- up the Lit

speaker: SS
words: tle Canyon- that's taken off at Peck, right up that way up to Moler.

speaker: SS
words: And built a town there and at that time there was three saloons, one

speaker: SS
words: hotel, a flour mill,a stock receiving station and scales, two general

speaker: SS
words: stores and one hardware, church and school and I don't know what else.

speaker: SS
words: But anyhow after the survey was made by Volmer from Lewiston to Grangeville

speaker: SS
words: up the Culdesac and Craigmont area up there, which is

speaker: SS
words: the present road, why he withdrew his building a railroad. It was

speaker: SS
words: supposed to be headquartered at Moler. We were three and a half miles

speaker: SS
words: north and east of Moler; the ranch.

speaker: SS
words: So it would have been a good location, the railroad would have gone

speaker: SS
words: right through there.

speaker: WM
words: Oh, yes. And it would have been the easiest road to travel; the rail

speaker: WM
words: road. But, they'd had to cross another canyon, the Lawyers Canyon to

speaker: WM
words: get to Grangeville.

speaker: SS
words: Was that a fight between Moler and Volmer to get the railroad by the

speaker: SS
words: route that they wanted?

speaker: WM
words: No, it wasn't much of a scrap to it. The Camas Prairie Railroad, I

speaker: WM
words: think it was then the Union Pacific, just decided to build it, that's

speaker: WM
words: all there was to it.

speaker: SS
words: Volrner happened to be at the right place at the right time there.

speaker: WM
words: Well, he wasn t, because Volmer himself built two or three other tamsites.

speaker: WM
words: None of them survived. He built a townsite, which is now Crai£

speaker: WM
words: mont on the northeast side of the highway, and the highway cut right

speaker: WM
words: through. Then Ilo, which used to be an old town about two miles west

speaker: WM
words: of Craigmont, it folded up and moved to Craigmont, which was later cal

speaker: WM
words: led Craigmont, and then the battle was on who would be the city and

speaker: WM
words: what would be the name of it, whether it'd be Ilo or Volmer. They fin

speaker: WM
words: ally decided on the name of Craigmont.

speaker: SS
words: Neither one. That happened to Troy; it was originally called Volmer

speaker: SS
words: but the people changed the name. I've heard that.

speaker: WM
words: Yes, that's right. And there's a lot of towns up there at that same-finne

speaker: WM
words: that Moler was there- there was a town called Frazier. That's due west

speaker: WM
words: of Moler, about four miles. Then there was another town about two

speaker: WM
words: miles east of what is now Craigmont called Dublin. And then this side

speaker: WM
words: of there; this side of Craigmont was Reubens and a little town called

speaker: WM
words: Gifford and a community called Summit and another community called Look

speaker: WM
words: out. They were all in a row between Craigmont and- well, actually and

speaker: WM
words: Peck, and that was called Big Canyon.

speaker: SS
words: Well, when you first moved there, was it all just the beginning of the

speaker: SS
words: farming in there.

speaker: WM
words: No big farms; they'd farmed two or three years, I think they had, be

speaker: WM
words: cause we bought- Dad bought the ranch and there was a binder on it, it

speaker: WM
words: wasn't any good but there was a pair of horses, team of horses left

speaker: WM
words: there from a runaway on this binder. They killed the other two horses

speaker: WM
words: and these two survived. One of 'em was a race horse. He had cut hisbarbed

speaker: WM
words: wire, practically his neck- clear to the cord, you could see

speaker: WM
words: his neck. And that horse stayed there and worked on the ranch like

speaker: WM
words: any other horse til he was thirty years old. The damndest thing

speaker: WM
words: ever saw! He was just a perfect animal. He had a chunk cut out of his

speaker: WM
words: shoulder and neck that you could put your hands in it; put a board across

speaker: WM
words: and never touch it.

speaker: WM
words: Oh, yes. Lots of cayuse horses; Indian ponies. This wasn't, it was a

speaker: WM
words: half Hamiltonian, got mixed up with the Indians someway. There was

speaker: WM
words: lots of wild horses. In fact, a chum of mine and myself; I was thir

speaker: WM
words: teen and he was twelve, we broke one summer eleven head of wild horses

speaker: WM
words: that come from a man named Hunt from a little community called Winona

speaker: WM
words: that's up from Kamiah on the hill. And these were wild horses, actual

speaker: WM
words: ly. One of 'em nine years old, never had a rope on it.

speaker: SS
words: You were thirteen?

speaker: WM
words: I was thirteen and my chum was twelve. We broke that eleven head that

speaker: WM
words: summer to work and about two or three of 'em to ride. We worked 'em

speaker: WM
words: that summer. We had three older horses; one of 'em was actually an old

speaker: WM
words: horse and the other two was four year olds. So we had quite a crew!

speaker: SS
words: Can you tell me how you went about breaking them. The way you did it.

speaker: WM
words: Well, we had no grain, no feed, so we turned 'em out at night, but we

speaker: WM
words: fed 'em grain in the morning. So, we kept one horse to ride and we were

speaker: WM
words: on the Looking Glass Indian Reservation ranch, right on top of the hill

speaker: WM
words: from Kamiah. The Mc Leods were the people that owned the property,

speaker: WM
words: but not the Indian land, this is Indian land. We turned 'em out at

speaker: WM
words: night and go drive 'em into this corral and then we'd lasso 'em. We

speaker: WM
words: built a corral about eight foot high out of timbers that we'd cut down

speaker: WM
words: and we roped these horses and snub 'em up to the posts and then we'd

speaker: WM
words: get up on the rail and drop the harness down on top of 'em. We had a

speaker: WM
words: stick with a nail in it, and we'd reach through and get the belly but

speaker: WM
words: ton and pull it through and fasten that, and we had long chains that

speaker: WM
words: we'd put on the butt chains- part of 'em was butt chains, part of 'em

speaker: WM
words: wasn't- but they were long tugs that we could get far enough back that

speaker: WM
words: we wasn't in too much danger of being kicked. But we treated these

speaker: WM
words: horses very nicely; never had much trouble, only they were wild. Hail

speaker: WM
words: storm come up one day while they was in the field and they just turned

speaker: WM
words: around with their backs to the storm and they come right over a

speaker: WM
words: still hitched up. Never hurt a one. So, then we worked them that sum

speaker: WM
words: mer on the plow, we had six head on a two bottom gang and three on a

speaker: WM
words: footburner plow. And the two of us changed off, one to the other, get

speaker: WM
words: tired walking. And that was in 1910 and '11.

speaker: SS
words: How did you come by that in the first place?

speaker: WM
words: Man by the name of Mc Leod that we worked forchis son and me, I was

speaker: WM
words: his neighbor in Nez Perce School, at that time, and he traded a house

speaker: WM
words: and lot there in Nez Perce for thirteen head of horses.

speaker: SS
words: To a—?

speaker: WM
words: Man by the name of Hunt Winona. And we went over there and drove 'em

speaker: WM
words: across the canyon and then broke 'em to work that summer. Try that

speaker: WM
words: some time!

speaker: SS
words: Sounds like you started pretty young.

speaker: WM
words: Well, people were— kids were men those days at that age. Because I

speaker: WM
words: started my first threshing run when I was eleven years old. I cut

speaker: WM
words: bands on the horse power. Had seven teams on the big three power

speaker: WM
words: driven by a tumble rod from the power plant to the thresher. And I

speaker: WM
words: worked on that when I was eleven and twelve. And when I was thirteen

speaker: WM
words: I drove bundle wagon for the threshing outfit. And then the next yearno,

speaker: WM
words: two years after that, I drove what they called header box; grain

speaker: WM
words: come out of the binder, you know.

speaker: SS
words: What did you think of that at that time, you know, the threshing out

speaker: SS
words: fit, the whole business? It was quite an operation.

speaker: WM
words: Well, it was. Of course, a kid like me, eleven to thirteen years old-

speaker: WM
words: I was more proud to be in the threshing crew, than if I'd a made ten

speaker: WM
words: touchdowns on the football field! Kids at that time were trying to be

speaker: WM
words: grownup men. There was a different atmosphere, different thought about

speaker: WM
words: youngsters; when they got about fifteen years old, those days, as

speaker: WM
words: I remember it, they thought they were men. You know a kid about that

speaker: WM
words: age he thinks he knows a lot, but he finds out later he didn't. And

speaker: WM
words: that's the way I was, tool

speaker: SS
words: Was that threshing crew mostly local men from the area?

speaker: WM
words: Oh, yeah, mostly. What they usually done, that was a small rig, a

speaker: WM
words: twenty-eight inch separator, and there was about sixteen or seventeen

speaker: WM
words: in the crew. But it was mostly neighbors and their sons, or nephews

speaker: WM
words: or whatever it might be; people lived in the neighborhood. They

speaker: WM
words: did hire some outside people.

speaker: SS
words: Did you just thresh the neighborhood? Or did they go outside?

speaker: WM
words: Oh, well, probably within a distance of maybe five, six miles.

speaker: SS
words: Did you have a cookwagon with you, or did you go home?

speaker: WM
words: Not at first, not when we had the horsepower rig, we didn't. We had

speaker: WM
words: the horsepower rig two years and then we bought an engine and then we

speaker: WM
words: had a cookhouse.

speaker: SS
words: Why did that make any difference?

speaker: WM
words: Well, first place it was faster and they had more horses to move with.

speaker: WM
words: And the bundle wagons'd usually leave- the bundle wagons, a couple of

speaker: WM
words: 'em and hook the teams from them onto the cookhouse. That was the

speaker: WM
words: main thing, to get moved. Later on I became. steam rig and it was my job to move the

speaker: WM
words: cookhouse and supply water for

speaker: WM
words: the threshing outfit; the horses and the steam engines.

speaker: SS
words: Did you try to be careful when you moved the cookhouse?

speaker: WM
words: Oh, yes, you had to be careful, because you were going across plowed

speaker: WM
words: ground.

speaker: SS
words: And all the stuff they had—

speaker: WM
words: Well, they didn't have much to cook with, it was pretty well taken care

speaker: WM
words: of. I never had a bit of trouble, I hauled the cookhouse for three

speaker: WM
words: years, and I never had any trouble at all, except I had an old balky

speaker: WM
words: horse once in a while, that it'd get too heavy he'd balk, but I knew

speaker: WM
words: how to handle it so I got moved alright.

speaker: SS
words: How much team work was there on a crew like that? Would the guys have

speaker: SS
words: to work very close together to coordinate things real close, to get

speaker: SS
words: things done?

speaker: WM
words: Ohhh, yes. Pretty much so. We had five bundle wagons, and that took

speaker: WM
words: from two to three horses on each one, so that'd be ten- about twelve

speaker: WM
words: horses there, and there was fourteen horses on the horsepower, so that

speaker: WM
words: would be twenty-six head there. And the wa|er team was four, that's

speaker: WM
words: thirty head. That's quite a bunch of horses on a threshing outfit.

speaker: WM
words: And nobody wanted the threshers when it rained, because they had to

speaker: WM
words: feed 'em.

speaker: SS
words: As long as it rained?

speaker: WM
words: Well, yeah.

speaker: SS
words: Stayed there?

speaker: WM
words: Yeah.

speaker: SS
words: Did that happen very much?

speaker: WM
words: Oh, yes. In- oh, I forget what year it was- it rained quite a little

speaker: WM
words: bit in the fall, and it cost some of the farmers a little bit of money.

speaker: WM
words: Course, it didn't cost 'em money, only they couldn't make any money,

speaker: WM
words: they couldn't sell it, because the horses ate it up. And then they had

speaker: WM
words: to feed the men. Of course, a lot of 'em went home, but what extras

speaker: WM
words: they did hire, well, they usually drew some of their pay and went to

speaker: WM
words: town, celebrated for a day or two. So, it wasn't too expensive as far

speaker: WM
words: as the feeding of the people, was concerned.

speaker: SS
words: Was there much chance for socializing with the neighbors during that

speaker: SS
words: time? Like in the evening and that kind of thing.

speaker: WM
words: No. When you got through one of them days, you went to bed right now!

speaker: WM
words: You were just simply worn out, that's all. There was no socializing;

speaker: WM
words: very little.

speaker: SS
words: So it was real hard work.

speaker: WM
words: You just stayed there and slept in the haystacks. Tickled to death

speaker: WM
words: to do it.

speaker: SS
words: How long a day would that be then?

speaker: WM
words: How long a day?

speaker: SS
words: Yeah, of working.

speaker: WM
words: Well, we was up at five. You were supposed to have your horses harnes

speaker: WM
words: sed and fed before you went to the cookhouse for something to eat, or

speaker: WM
words: the house where you was working at. And you started the machine at

speaker: WM
words: seven; some of 'em got started at six. The horsepower rigs they couldn'tthe

speaker: WM
words: horses couldn't take it. They'd usually wait til about seven. The

speaker: WM
words: steam engines, they could get off at about six.

speaker: SS
words: Then how long would you go? Would you go right til sunset?

speaker: WM
words: Usually about six o'clock, but that's sunset then.

speaker: SS
words: You worked really hard. Hard enough to really do you in by evening.

speaker: WM
words: Well, it was hard work because all your jobs were not simplified.

speaker: WM
words: When you worked sewing sacks on the threshing outfit, you handled every

speaker: WM
words: one of those sacks. You sewed it up, picked it up and carried it over

speaker: WM
words: to the pile. When you carry 180 sacks a daythat's sewing and jig 'em,

speaker: WM
words: jolt 'em down you know, that's not for— a lot of small fellows did it

speaker: WM
words: but they were tough, they knew how to do it. Knew how to put it up on

speaker: WM
words: their knee, pick up. You take these sissies you got here now in these

speaker: WM
words: colleges I don't think any of the football teams they got- I don't

speaker: WM
words: think the players could do it. You don't work, you don't know what

speaker: WM
words: work is. But, you get up in the morning, you take care of your hor

speaker: WM
words: ses; feed 'em, take care of yourself the best you can, maybe you shave,

speaker: WM
words: maybe you don't. Cold; kick the snow off your blankets or frost.

speaker: WM
words: You don't wash your face very much, either! I worked several years on

speaker: WM
words: the threshing. The things that bothered us those days was building

speaker: WM
words: roads, because in those days there was no roads. So every man, by law,

speaker: WM
words: had to pay a poll tax and you could work out your poll tax with a team

speaker: WM
words: and horses, and when I was hired out it was usually my job to take the

speaker: WM
words: boss's team and go out and take his place on the road. And we usually

speaker: WM
words: had scrapers, what they called two-horse or four-horse Fresnoes. Do

speaker: WM
words: you know what they are?

speaker: SS
words: You becter explain to me; a Fresno.

speaker: WM
words: A Fresno is a long scraper. It's about, oh, about five foot long, I'd

speaker: WM
words: say, oh, about six, and two horses hitched to each end and a big, long

speaker: WM
words: iron handle. Well, you drove these four horses the best you could up

speaker: WM
words: to where you was going to load and you take hold of this iron and lift

speaker: WM
words: it up and it'd scoop up the dirt, rocks, or whatever it was, then you

speaker: WM
words: pull it back down. Now, if you think that's easy, just try om e'

speaker: WM
words: And especially— it is fairly easy if the horses are trained to pull

speaker: WM
words: even, but the worst thing I got into, I had two big black mares, weighed

speaker: WM
words: about 1,400 pounds apiece, and two little mule Jennies, and they weighed

speaker: WM
words: about 850, and they were on one end of this scraper and the big horses

speaker: WM
words: were on the other end, and when the little mules would feel that bite

speaker: WM
words: of that scoop, they'd pull ahead real quick, throw the weight and all

speaker: WM
words: the load on the big team. Soon as they got the thing loaded, which

speaker: WM
words: their end wasn't loaded, why they backed up and went right along as a

speaker: WM
words: team. And you say a mule ain't smart! You try it; work with them a

speaker: WM
words: while (Chuckles) And this big iron bar .come around and hit me if

speaker: WM
words: I was foolish enough to stand in front of it- break your leg, break

speaker: WM
words: your bones, or anything, because the weight on that big long iron

speaker: WM
words: bar about five or six foot long hit you there with those teams pul

speaker: WM
words: ling against it, there's a leverage there, just knock the tar out

speaker: WM
words: of you! Anybody that's ever drove a Fresno knows what I'm talking

speaker: WM
words: about.

speaker: SS
words: How much of that you do in a day?

speaker: WM
words: Oh, well, we worked about ten hours. I think that was a day then;

speaker: WM
words: ten hours on the road.

speaker: SS
words: On a stretch road, how long would you have to put in on your poll tax;

speaker: SS
words: how many days of work was that?

speaker: WM
words: Well, it depends on how much land you had. So much an acre. I don't

speaker: WM
words: know what the assessment was, but you was assessed so many dollars and

speaker: WM
words: then you got so much a day for a man and his team credit.

speaker: SS
words: Was that how most people did it; was work it out on the road?

speaker: SS
words: Oh, a lot of 'em did, yes, 'cause they didn't have any money. Usually

speaker: SS
words: the work on the roads was done in the late spring, and by that time the

speaker: SS
words: winter's over, you didn't have any money. That's all. You just didn't

speaker: SS
words: have it, so they worked out, most of it. And that's how these roads

speaker: SS
words: all over this country were built; working out their poll tax. The old

speaker: SS
words: Kamiah grade from the Indian Reservation there, Looking Glass and the

speaker: SS
words: Catch-Catch place down to Kamiah was built by that kind of labor.

speaker: SS
words: You get out there and handle a pick and a shovel, and if you don't know

speaker: SS
words: how to handle a pick or a shovel or sledgehammer you got an education

speaker: SS
words: coming! I got one. I'd slam that sledgehammer down with all the might

speaker: SS
words: I had and I'd slam it down and I didn't give any with it, and my wrists

speaker: SS
words: was just almost paralyzed. After about two or three days I let the

speaker: SS
words: sledgehammer do it's own hitting and I'd hold it loose; didn't hurt

speaker: SS
words: then. You work on those roads like the men used to do and that was a

speaker: SS
words: job. That road from Nez Perce to Orofino; if you've ever been over

speaker: SS
words: it here lately you'll see what I mean. That original road's still

speaker: SS
words: there. And the one from Nez Perce to Greer, there's another one.

speaker: SS
words: mean, besides this poll tax business, was it all regular

speaker: SS
words: road jobs that Me could get?

speaker: SS
words: No. Taxes was very limited. About one load of wheat paid our taxes,

speaker: SS
words: on our place. KThose days, all the ranches up there were 160 acres,

speaker: SS
words: now they're about 2,000. One man does more work now than the whole

speaker: SS
words: neighborhood ever did before. There's a lot of difference between

speaker: SS
words: living now and living then; just a hell of a lot.

speaker: SS
words: What was the neighborhood like when you were growing up then? Was there

speaker: SS
words: much of a real neighborhood right there where you lived?

speaker: SS
words: Yes, there was a man and a family on every 160; almost a family on

speaker: SS
words: every 160 acres.

speaker: SS
words: Did that mean that you were very close to the neighbors; that you knew

speaker: SS
words: them real well?

speaker: SS
words: Well, half a mile. Quarter section, 160 acres is half a mile, so you

speaker: SS
words: was a half a mile from your closest neighbor. You may have three or

speaker: SS
words: more around you.

speaker: SS
words: Well how much did that mean that you associated with them?

speaker: SS
words: Oh, not too much. You didn't associate maybe once in two months, maybe,

speaker: SS
words: on a Sundays. Very little evening association; practically none.

speaker: SS
words: The nearest we had to associations was one of the first telephones. My

speaker: SS
words: dad helped run the line from Peck to Moler, and it was run on the bar

speaker: SS
words: bed wire fences. It would follow a half a mile of fence along the

speaker: SS
words: quarter section; 160 acres, if there was a gateway you went up over it,

speaker: SS
words: across and down. And the cattle was always breaking the fence down

speaker: SS
words: so your telephone service was not very good] Because, maybe down the

speaker: SS
words: road eight miles somebody run into the fence, no connection. Now

speaker: SS
words: our association then- my dad was quite progressive in some ways; we

speaker: SS
words: had one of the first phonographs, one of these Edisons with the round

speaker: SS
words: records, yah long,and about that big around, and one of these big horns

speaker: SS
words: that come out. And at a certain time in the evening, it would ring on

speaker: SS
words: the telephone; crank, you know, and you could just hear the receivers,

speaker: SS
words: click, click, click all up and down the line, and we would turn on that

speaker: SS
words: phonograph into the telephone. And we'd have The Preacher and the Bear,

speaker: SS
words: and some violin, maybe, or tunes like The Devil's Dream or the Old Irish-

speaker: SS
words: Washwoman or Soldier's Joy, or some of those old-time tunes, they'd be

speaker: SS
words: played for the whole line to listen! And that time you couldn't call

speaker: SS
words: anybody, cause everybody had their phones down. And that's about as

speaker: SS
words: much socializing they did, excepting-

speaker: SS
words: Four miles-

speaker: WM
words: About four miles apart. You'd have a schoolhouse about four-five miles,

speaker: WM
words: maybe closer than that. And in this community with one family on every

speaker: WM
words: 160 acres, let's see, you had about fifteen families to a schoolhouse.

speaker: WM
words: And they had what is known as literary societies. You put on your own

speaker: WM
words: plays, debates, musical programs, religious programs and everything like

speaker: WM
words: that. Box socials. That's what socializing was done those days. I

speaker: WM
words: remember my dad and mother had a play that they had made up pretty much

speaker: WM
words: themselves, although they got the principle part of it out of a maga

speaker: WM
words: zine, "A Slow Train Through Arkansas". You had the old passenger coach

speaker: WM
words: and the kids trailed along behind, the old maid with her bird in a cage

speaker: WM
words: and all that kind of stuff, and this slow train to Arkansas, it would

speaker: WM
